agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with explicit goals (competitiveness, profitability, automation) but leaves most implementation details and business constraints vague or implied.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of current delivery request volume relative to available capacity in a given area/time window — definition not yet specified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or unreasonable pricing — thresholds not yet defined" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied but not stated — the reference price from which dynamic adjustments are made" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to a delivery price as a function of current demand level" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjusted in real time based on current demand levels",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core product behavior — a business policy decision about how pricing works. A VP could decide to pause dynamic pricing or switch to manual adjustments without corrupting system state.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand level, the system recalculates and applies a new price within a defined real-time SLA (SLA undefined)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand is high, prices must increase relative to the baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. Leadership could decide to hold prices flat during high demand (e.g., during a natural disaster for PR reasons).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand classified as 'high', the price adjustment factor is > 1.0 (or additive delta > 0)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand is low, prices must decrease relative to the baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. Leadership could decide to hold a price floor rather than discount during low demand.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand classified as 'low', the price adjustment factor is < 1.0 (or additive delta < 0)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must never cause the final delivery price to be zero or negative",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative price for a delivery service is physically and financially nonsensical. It would corrupt revenue calculations, create arbitrage opportunities, and break invoicing. No legitimate business decision would allow paying customers to deliver.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any demand level and any price adjustment, the resulting delivery price is strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must enforce upper and lower bounds on the adjusted price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The CEO said 'probably' guardrails — this is a policy control. The specific thresholds are configurable business decisions, not physical constraints.",
      "testable_assertion": "The adjusted price is clamped to a configured [min_price, max_price] range before being presented to the customer"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The pricing system must operate in a fully automated manner without manual intervention for individual price decisions",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is an operational policy. The business could decide to require manual approval for certain thresholds or regions without system corruption.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are computed and applied programmatically without requiring human approval per transaction"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing strategy must balance competitiveness (not pricing out of the market) with profitability (covering costs and generating margin)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a system constraint. The balance point is a judgment call that will shift over time.",
      "testable_assertion": "UNTESTABLE as stated — requires decomposition into specific margin floors and competitive price ceilings"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A delivery price quoted to a customer must be the price honored for that delivery (price lock on acceptance)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Implied by real-time pricing in a transactional system. If a customer accepts a quoted price and the system charges a different amount, this breaks contractual integrity and likely violates consumer protection law. The quoted-equals-charged relationship is a data consistency invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a customer accepts a delivery quote at price P, the invoiced amount for that delivery equals P regardless of subsequent demand changes"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., 10x normal) during a natural disaster or emergency", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails cap the price at the configured maximum. Additionally, regulatory or ethical overrides may need to suppress surge pricing entirely — this is not addressed in the spec." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero in a region", "expected_behavior": "Price decreases but must not go below the configured floor price (which must be > 0). System must not divide by zero or produce undefined behavior." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid demand oscillation (demand flips high/low every few seconds)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — the spec does not address price stability, dampening, or minimum hold periods. Without these, customers may see prices flickering, eroding trust." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple simultaneous quote requests during a demand transition", "expected_behavior": "Each customer receives the price valid at their quote time. Race conditions must not produce inconsistent pricing for the same demand snapshot." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail thresholds are misconfigured (min > max, or max < cost)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — the system needs validation of guardrail configuration to prevent self-contradictory constraints." },
    { "scenario": "Cost of delivery exceeds the dynamically adjusted price during low demand", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — the spec says 'profitable' but does not define whether per-delivery margin must always be positive or if aggregate profitability is sufficient." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand and increase volume during low demand. However, poorly calibrated guardrails could either leave money on the table (caps too low) or drive customers to competitors (caps too high). Per-delivery margin risk during low demand is unaddressed.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with low-latency computation, and configuration management for guardrails. Introduces operational complexity: monitoring, alerting on pricing anomalies, and incident response for pricing failures.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price surges may cause negative sentiment. Price drops may increase adoption during off-peak. Quote stability and transparency are critical UX concerns not addressed in the spec.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Regulatory risk from surge pricing (some jurisdictions restrict price gouging during emergencies). Reputational risk from perceived unfairness. Financial risk from pricing below cost. Technical risk from real-time system failures causing either no pricing or wrong pricing.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers (senders)", "impact": "Variable delivery costs; potential sticker shock during high demand; potential savings during low demand" },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Demand-driven pricing may affect delivery volume and earnings — relationship between dynamic pricing and courier compensation is undefined" },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue forecasting becomes more complex; need visibility into pricing distribution and margin analysis" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability; need clear policies on price disputes and refunds" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price gouging regulations, consumer protection compliance, and pricing transparency requirements" },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model to customers transparently; competitive positioning changes" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement, not a specification. It establishes directional intent (prices go up/down with demand, automated, guardrails) but defines zero concrete thresholds, no demand measurement methodology, no guardrail values, no pricing formula, no cost-floor relationship, no regulatory stance, and no price-lock semantics. Almost every business rule extracted requires significant assumptions or decomposition before it is implementable. The word 'probably' for guardrails signals even that concept is uncommitted.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices adjust based on demand (directional intent is clear)",
      "High demand → higher prices, low demand → lower prices (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Final price must be positive (physical/financial invariant)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'demand' — how is it measured, over what time window, per what geography?",
      "What are the guardrail thresholds — or even whether guardrails are confirmed ('probably')",
      "What is the pricing formula or adjustment mechanism (multiplicative surge? additive? tiered?)",
      "Whether per-delivery profitability is required or only aggregate profitability",
      "Price-lock semantics on quote acceptance",
      "Regulatory and legal constraints on dynamic pricing",
      "How demand signals interact with other pricing factors (distance, weight, urgency, customer tier)",
      "Whether courier compensation changes with dynamic pricing"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base/reference price exists from which adjustments are made",
      "Price must be locked once a customer accepts a quote",
      "Guardrails mean hard upper and lower bounds on the adjusted price",
      "'Real time' means within seconds, not minutes or hours",
      "'Demand' refers to delivery request volume relative to courier capacity in a service area"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No definition of 'demand' — is it request volume per area per hour? Ratio of requests to available couriers? Predictive or reactive? The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is completely unspecified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Data Science", "Product", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Request-to-capacity ratio in a geofenced zone over a rolling time window", "Absolute request volume compared to historical baseline", "Predictive demand model based on time, weather, events"],
      "recommendation": "Require Product to define demand measurement methodology with Data Science before any implementation begins"
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No pricing formula or adjustment mechanism specified. Is this a multiplier (1.0x–2.5x)? Additive surcharge? Tiered brackets? The shape of the price-demand curve is entirely undefined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance", "Product"],
      "options": ["Multiplicative surge factor (e.g., Uber-style)", "Additive surcharge on base price", "Discrete pricing tiers mapped to demand bands"],
      "recommendation": "Require Product and Finance to define the pricing model with concrete examples before implementation"
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrails are described as 'probably' — not confirmed, and no thresholds specified. Without guardrails, the system could produce arbitrarily high or below-cost prices.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance", "Product"],
      "options": ["Hard min/max price bounds per service type", "Maximum percentage deviation from base price", "Both absolute bounds and percentage caps"],
      "recommendation": "Guardrails must be confirmed and defined with specific values before launch. Recommend mandatory min (>= cost) and max (regulatory/reputational ceiling) bounds."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory or legal review mentioned. Dynamic/surge pricing is regulated in some jurisdictions (price gouging laws during emergencies, consumer protection requirements for price transparency). This is a significant compliance risk.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Executive team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Proceed with legal review before launch", "Implement emergency pricing override kill-switch as day-one requirement"],
      "recommendation": "Mandatory legal review of dynamic pricing model in all operating jurisdictions before any customer-facing launch. Emergency override capability is a hard requirement."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Relationship between dynamic delivery price and courier compensation is undefined. If customer-facing prices surge but courier pay stays flat, there are labor fairness concerns. If courier pay also surges, the margin model changes.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Couriers/Drivers", "Finance", "HR/Operations"],
      "options": ["Courier compensation is independent of dynamic pricing", "Couriers receive a percentage of the surge premium", "Courier pay has its own demand-based model"],
      "recommendation": "Define courier compensation relationship to dynamic pricing before launch — this affects both unit economics and courier retention"
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No mention of price transparency to customers. Will customers see 'surge pricing in effect'? Will they see the base price vs. adjustment? Transparency directly impacts trust and may be legally required.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Product", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["Show only final price", "Show base price + surge markup", "Show demand level indicator with final price"],
      "recommendation": "Recommend transparent pricing display showing demand level and its effect on price — builds trust and may be legally required in some markets"
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
